ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for 'Naan Xiang Nang'. Mapped to closest wheat-based noodle dish class. Dish is a Northern Chinese restaurant preparation served in Singapore.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat noodle dish — composite prepared dish with sauce and protein
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (pasta and noodle dishes) used as closest defensible anchor. No specific code exists for this Northern Chinese noodle pouch preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl serving of approximately 400 g, typical for a single-person restaurant portion of this Northern Chinese noodle dish as served in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry (SR Legacy 20113) and composite wheat noodle dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Naan Xiang Nang' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy wheat noodle dish composites (FDC IDs 20113, 18033) and cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo data for stir-fried noodle dishes. Input values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 7.0 g, fat 6.25 g, carbohydrate 18.75 g per 100 g) are consistent with USDA composite estimates for stir-fried wheat noodle dishes with minced meat and vegetables and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, water, and several micronutrients set to -1 where not reported in the adapted source composite.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~52, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Naan Xiang Nang'. GI of 52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI ~52, glucose reference scale), which is the closest analogous food. Stir-frying and the addition of protein and fat from minced meat may modestly lower the effective GI; 52 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 75.0) / 100 = 39.0 (carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 18.75 × 4 = 75.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size to no more than 200 g per meal to keep sodium intake below 900 mg per sitting.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat noodles contribute 18.75 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.25 g dietary fibre, yielding a low fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio of 6.7%, and an estimated GI of 52 with a high glycemic load of 39 per standard 400 g serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the serving to 150–200 g, request additional non-starchy vegetables such as cabbage and carrots to increase fibre, and monitor postprandial blood glucose carefully given the high glycemic load of a full bowl.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0 g per 100 g and sodium 450 mg per 100 g from minced red meat and soy sauce; cholesterol 17.5 mg per 100 g; trans fat 0.05 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request a lean protein substitution such as chicken breast or tofu in place of minced pork or lamb, ask for reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, and limit portion size to manage total saturated fat and sodium intake per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced red meat (pork or lamb) provides arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; sodium at 450 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute minced pork or lamb with chicken or tofu, request reduced soy sauce, and be aware that chilli, if added, is a nightshade ingredient that some RA patients report sensitivity to.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Naan Xiang Nang (馕香囊) is a Northern Chinese-influenced wheat noodle dish served at Bei Jiang (Northern Frontier) restaurant in Singapore. The name combines 'naan' (馕), a Central Asian and Northwestern Chinese leavened flatbread traditionally baked in a tandoor-style oven and common in Xinjiang cuisine, with 'xiang nang' (香囊), meaning fragrant pouch, suggesting either a flatbread element served alongside or incorporated into the noodle preparation, or noodles shaped or presented in a pouch-like form. The dish is prepared by stir-frying or tossing wheat noodles with a savoury soy-based sauce, minced pork or lamb, and vegetables such as cabbage and carrots, seasoned with ginger, cumin, and optional chilli. The result is a hearty, umami-rich noodle bowl with a chewy noodle texture, mild spice, and aromatic depth from cumin and ginger. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 7.0 g protein, 6.25 g fat, and 18.75 g carbohydrate with 1.25 g dietary fibre and 450 mg sodium. The high sodium content (450 mg per 100 g) is a concern for hypertension and rheumatoid arthritis management. The moderate glycemic index (~52) combined with a high glycemic load per standard 400 g serving (GL ~39) makes portion control important for Type 2 Diabetes. The red meat component contributes saturated fat and arachidonic acid relevant to cardiovascular and inflammatory disease management. This dish is culturally significant as a representation of Northern Chinese wheat-based culinary traditions adapted for the Singapore dining context.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 'Naan Xiang Nang' exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy wheat noodle dish composites and are consistent with the input values provided. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled wheat noodles, University of Sydney GI Database). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy wheat noodle dish composites (FDC IDs 20113, 18033) as no exact approved-source entry exists for this dish. Input values retained as consistent with adapted source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, water_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, and vitamin panel data for this specific Northern Chinese restaurant noodle preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of April 2026. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from boiled wheat noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 4. Halal status is uncertain as the dish may contain pork; flagged is_halal as false pending restaurant confirmation. 5. The dish name 'Naan香囊' from the input combines a transliteration of the Chinese character 馕 (naan flatbread) with 香囊 (fragrant pouch); the canonical English name has been expanded for clarity. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.05 g per 100 g is retained from input as a trace value consistent with minor trans fat from cooking oils; this is a plausible trace amount for a stir-fried dish.
